title: Melissa Wankiewicz -Hiring Manager Interview - Jason | Managing Editor, GrowthX Publication
meeting_title: Melissa Wankiewicz -Hiring Manager Interview - Jason | Managing Editor, GrowthX Publication

transcript.text: Thank you.
Hello.
Hi, Melissa.
How's it going?
I'm good, thank you.
How are you?
Doing good.
My kid is sick, so he's been home like all week, so that's sucked, but it is Friday.
I'm sorry to hear that.
Hopefully he feels better or they feel better soon.
Definitely something going around, though.
I feel like everybody in my office has been taken out lately.
Oh, man.
Do you work like in an office or is it you guys?
I'm hybrid.
So Monday to Wednesday, I'm in office.
Okay, got it.
Yeah, but I really appreciate you taking the time.
Marilia said lots of great things.
So I guess typically I just like spend some time explaining everything we're working on and like who we're, you know, what we're trying to do and why we reached out.
Yeah.
Yeah, then we can go from there.
I mean, I think very much so this meeting is just to like try to give you more information, I think, just to see if this is like interesting.
And then obviously we'll have to hear more about kind of the stuff you do as well.
Does that work?
Yeah, absolutely.
Sounds good to me.
Cool.
So I, I, I'm Jason.
I lead growth here at GrowthX.
And I don't know if, again, really gave you the whole spiel, but I mean, as a company, we're trying.
doing.
To essentially build a platform so people can kind of work with AI, you know, the same way that like engineers work with AI.
And we want kind of marketers, salespeople go to market to have like a platform where they, you know, feel AI native and they're just having a lot of leverage to what they do.

And our kind of very odd roundabout way of doing that is like, we've built this agency, essentially like a regular, you know, growth agency to prove out like what it takes to kind of build that platform.
So today, like that agency, it's called GrowthX.
We have like maybe 70 customers where I think we just hit like 13 million in revenue because usually like these engagements are pretty big, you know, like it's kind of a full services engagement.
And then for our customers, we essentially help them build awareness through SEO, through like AEO now, getting LLMs to like talk about your product.
By just creating like a ton of content.
And our whole model is like, you know, we, our whole model is like, we essentially build these like machines that get really good at creating a certain type of topic.
And then machines are like, mostly AI, but like with some experts in the loop.

So for example, we do, we do plenty of like the, you know, typical kind of SEO content, but we also do a lot of like, like, for example, Webflow is a client and we do all their developer docs that have to do with integration.
So if you go to like webflow.com slash integrations, like all those guides we're kind of doing at scale.
think we do like 30 of them a week.
Then we go back and we like refresh them.
And then, yeah, so it's a company.
That's what we're trying to do.
We're like releasing products or doing services.
And essentially the like core of our marketing is this community that we've built where we essentially just go and we like try to teach teach people.
How to do this themselves.
The whole angle is like, you know, once you learn how to actually do it, then you realize it's like way too much work and like you'll never be able to hire the people that can do it.
So like you come and you find us.
And for this community, I could even show you, not sure if you looked at it, it's called AI Like Growth.
Mariela showed me during our first call.
So I took a peek.
Okay, cool.
Yeah, so for this community, mean, we do all sorts of different content.
It's like super early.
Like we haven't really even officially launched it.
We just kind of do like workshops and then we point people here for the recording.
But like, you know, we do workshops where we kind of show the different processes of how we create content.
We do like this little podcast where, you know, we have a lot of kind of friends and clients that like are marketing leaders that like go through this exact kind of process.
Like AI is changing everything.
Like, what are you doing?
You know, type of angle.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.

We have courses, have masterclass, and I think the help that I'm really looking for is somebody to own the content machine, both from what we're publishing in general, and to facilitate the creation of new content, whether that's writing it fully, or just bringing the right people into the room to pull that information out of people's minds, and then shaping the form of it, essentially.
And, yeah, and that's why we reached out.
think, like, compared to most content programs that, you know, companies run where you just publish some blog posts, I think this is, like, the content is almost the product here in a lot of ways.
People actually pay for this, which is, like, the crazy thing for me.
I think, like, I don't know, I think right now, like, 70, 60, 70 people, like, pay, like, 200 bucks a year for this, and we want to make it more expensive.
We're going to have, like, a flagship course, you know, and all that stuff.
So community, sorry.
The community is a paid community.
I think I want to structure it so there is like some sort of free tier.
We use this platform called Circle and it's like a little clunky to do that.
But I think like I would love for some things to be free.
Like maybe the last workshop is free.
But if you want the like, you know, the 10 other ones we've done, like you got to pay.
Yeah, but this is paid.
I guess a lot of reasons for it to be paid.
think like, you know, growthx, the service that ultimately this like funnels into like, it's quite expensive and we want to like keep the noise out of this.
think we want only people that are kind of like serious about wanting to learn about this stuff.
And it's also a good benchmark for us.
Like if people aren't willing to pay, then I guess the content we're creating isn't that valuable anyway.
Yeah, no, that makes a lot of sense.
Yeah, so that's, mean, I'll stop there.
And then my background, I had a growth at a few places.
I lead growth here.
So like I have a couple of clients and then kind of all the marketing.
It's like something I think about and I can go deeper into that if you're interested.
then, yeah, I've been head of growth.
I've been a founder, really technical personally, which seems like a nice fit now that everyone's using AI.
Yeah, and I've been here since March, so it hasn't been that long, but essentially the company only started a year ago.
I mean, in that time frame, you are a veteran, I guess.
Yeah, I'm probably one of the people that's been here the longest.
Yeah, but I'll stop there.
mean, any thoughts, any questions, anything not clear?
Yeah, I mean, I think it's definitely like an interesting concept.
And I'm actually really interested to learn more about like the product itself, because it's something that I've been playing around with myself too.
So like, or like, just like building content workflows in general.
So I'm just interested to see how, like, it's been.
And on your end, but yeah, no, I think everything that you said makes a lot of sense right now.
I'm sure I'll have more questions that come up, but right now everything's sounding good.
Okay.
Yeah.
I guess anything you can share about like what you've done?
I guess I just want to make sure like thing we're hiring for is, and then I guess if you are interested, you know, we'll have to hear about kind of where you're at now.
I looked at some of your website, you know, looked at Ask AI, but just in general, like what have you been working on?
What are you interested in doing?
Yeah, I can give you a little background on me and chat about like what I'm working on and what I'm thinking about and how I think about content.
But in general, I would say I describe myself as more of a demand focused content marketer.
And I say that just because I feel like I've been pretty lucky to either just going a better.
Sit within demand teams or work really closely with demand teams.

So in terms of like the way that I'm thinking about content, I feel like I'm often kind of like backing into ideas where I'm thinking about like distribution and then like, oh, this would be a really cool campaign.
How can we create content to support it?
And so but content has really always been kind of the core focus of what I've done in my roles, whether that's like awareness phase all the way through to like customer retention.
And I've, you know, managed small teams, been an individual contributor.
I'm definitely no stranger to a high growth startup environment.
So right now I'm a solo content marketer, really solo marketer at Ask.ai.
And so I've been there since March, April.
So which, you know, hasn't been super long, but I feel like because it's such a small team, I've really had the opportunity to kind of do a lot really quickly.
So one of my first.
Projects upon joining was building out like a agentic AI content workflow to kind of scale production just because I am a team of one.
So I use relevance AI to build that out, but it kind of like I feed in a bunch of like transcripts or like give it like keyword ideas.
And there was a whole bunch of agents that work together to kind of like build out content.
And so we've had some success with it.
Like we're ranking in like SEO and like for in GPT, which we weren't at all before, which has been really positive.

But yeah, I think just in terms of like why I was interested when Mariela reached out, obviously I feel like this role is, it's kind of uniquely interesting to me because it feels like kind of an intersection of, you know, a bunch of things that I'm interested in.
So it piqued my interest there.

But I think also just right now I'm kind of at a phase where my role at Ask AI, feel like we've had a few different leadership changeovers, which is kind of par for the course in a startup environment.
But I feel like my.
The role is going to be moving away from content a little bit.
So knowing that I'm someone who really wants to focus on content, I think that's kind of, you know, as if a content opportunity kind of comes across my desk, I'm going to explore it.
Yeah, that's really good context.
I mean, I think this role is interesting because essentially what you're doing is like evangelizing and writing about, it sounds like what you've already been doing with Relevance.
I think I've seen Relevance.
Is that the one where like they have little characters for like each other?
Yeah.
Yeah, I remember, I thought they're really big into like the sales side, like with sales agents, but I didn't know they also did like content writing.
It's pretty cool.
I feel like they've expanded.
Our like VP of sales is really into like just AI and everything.
So he was the one that kind of put me onto it, which it makes sense if it was a sales tool initially.
But yeah, no, it's really, it seems like they do everything.
Like you could build an agent for anything.
Okay.
have an agent builder.
Yeah, that is really cool.
I know there's a bunch of tools there and we're probably building something in that rough space.
I mean, yeah, I mean, it's like for the company itself, like the interesting thing about what we're doing is like we've wrapped the people as the product.
So like for a customer, they just like they're hiring us.
Essentially, they don't even see the platform, which is, you know, double edged sword in a lot of ways.
But like what it lets us do is like when we replace the people or when we bring scale to the people, like we get to still benefit from that.
Like I would say from when I started until now, like, I mean, we're definitely way more efficient, but customers still pay us the same amount.
You know, they don't look at us and be like, all right, you're automating all this stuff.
So like I'm going to pay you less now because like you're more efficient, you know, and I think that's kind of an interesting model.
Yeah, like Andreessen just had a podcast recently about like how software is eating labor and it's like kind of.
On this theme of like, like, you know, software is now starting to really replace like individuals, you know, which is scary, but yeah, like that's, that's how we're positioned.
Yeah.
And then the content part of it is like, like, if you look at the stuff we're publishing, like literally one of the videos that we created, that seemed pretty popular.
It was just like, I just sat there and I like used these cloud code or chat to PT projects to like, write like a documentation, like a piece of like documentation for a fictional company.

And yeah, so I think for this person, it's like, especially if you're already knowledgeable on that stuff, it'll kind of just feel like you're teaching people how to do stuff that, you know, you're learning yourself or like you're kind of well-versed in.
Yeah, I think that's really interesting.
I guess like, I was probably gonna, I was gonna say this question for the end, but I'll ask it now.
So I was thinking.

Just in terms of the community, and you mentioned this earlier in your initial introduction to it, but is there a concern that you get to a place where your customers figure out how to build this themselves?
Or is it more, you're being as educational as possible, but assuming that most people aren't going to want to go out of their way to build this kind of thing?
Yeah, I mean, that's, that's like, super interesting.
I think, so I come from DevTools, like doing marketing for those companies.
And like, the way they go about it is like, they have to respect their kind of customers and end users, because like, for those part, they're engineers and engineers are great at building.
So like, if you don't, like, if you try to keep that away from them, they almost like don't trust you.
Yeah, it seems like for marketing, that's never been the case of how people market, but like, we're trying to bring that here.
Like, think, firstly, I'm pretty confident that like,
Even if we show people everything, and I'm sure there's some things we'll always keep under wraps, but they really can't do it.
You would have to assemble a team that, honestly, no marketing team is even structured to set up, apart from a few.
If you look at our team, there's so many engineers on the team.

There's ML people, there's product managers working together with marketing people that, for whatever reason, their journey took them on a path that made them technical, and how they can kind of sit in the middle of these two things.
That's what it takes to build some of this stuff.
At least for now, we've always biased towards oversharing, almost to the point where it's scary, but it's never backfired.
It's never backfired yet, and I think, yeah, I mean, I think we will.
Continue to do that.

There's some things that we won't for, I mean, you know, like we won't share exactly what we're doing for customers and stuff like that, but I think, yeah, I think there will be a time where like our platform will be live and then it'll start to cannibalize like all these service contracts we have, I'm sure.
But, I mean, the goal was never to make this like services company like absolutely the thing this company does, you know.
So I think if it does come to that fork in the road, like we'll always lean towards like teaching people how to do it themselves and then letting them use our tool, basically.
Yeah, that makes sense.
I mean, I'm definitely a big fan of like the building in public approach and that kind of aligns with that.
So that makes sense.
Yeah.
But it is scary.
think sometimes, yeah, it does feel like we're sharing too much, but like, I don't know, really, it's like one in.
A hundred would even try to like do it.
Like I think people, yeah, people just, I mean, yeah, people are lazy.
And I guess like if you are a person or like a type of company that's used to hiring an agency anyway, like they, you're not going to like bother writing it yourself.
So why would you build a, bother like building things yourself?
So there, there's that.
But it seems like it, it seems like the trust it buys like beats the few companies that look at it and just be like, instead of hiring, I'll just do it, do it myself.
No, that's fair.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But I mean, I'm trying to think like what else I, I guess like a big part of this is like the creation of the, of the content.
So like some of it will be, you know, you writing and then some of it will be like somehow facilitating the creation of something.
So like we benefit from.
Just having like 70 clients now where we literally do this every day so we can kind of tap into that work and just show like real examples, real problems we work through.

Do you have any experience kind of like doing either of those things, like either creating for this sort of audience as like marketing leader, marketing operator audience, and then also on the other side of like bringing in like a domain expert to like facilitate creating something.
Maybe you don't, you know, fully have like that depth in.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I would say both for sure.
So when I was at Uberflip, I was there for a few years.
And so their audience, I'm not sure if you're familiar, it's like a kind of like a content management platform.
But that, you know, our primary audience was marketing to marketers.
And I loved that just because it's like you really can show like use like subject matter experts on your team to show like.
What you're building and kind of like pull back the curtain a little bit.
And I think that's a really interesting approach.

And I think it's also an interesting challenge because like marketers are pretty savvy to marketing and they're not going to like, you know, like I've spent a few years marketing to like HR professionals and support.
And I feel like sometimes those are easier to reach than marketers.
But I think like when you do get through to marketers and you build that trust, it resonates with them so much more.

So, yeah, when I was at Uberflip, we definitely did like a lot of like putting ourselves in like videos and like, you know, like campaigns and webinars and things where we were showcasing like what we were doing and tapping subject matter experts to do the same.

And I've kind of modeled that approach in every other company that I've worked for after that, where it's like, you know, maybe I'm not, you know, an HR professional, but I will like get like our head of people ops to like.

Go do a webinar or put together ghostwrite a newsletter or something like that and leveraging those people within the organization to kind of showcase what they're doing and build trust because those are also the kind of people who will have the networks that you're trying to reach.
So if we're showcasing a marketer's work, they're going to be reaching other marketers if they're posting it on LinkedIn or in that way too.
So, yeah, I would say that's definitely like the approach I like to take is leveraging subject matter experts to showcase what we're doing.
Cool.
Is there like a particular example of something like a little bit more substantial?
Like maybe it's a workshop, it's a course, it's a white paper that you've done like lately or before?
Yeah.
So what I was initially thinking about as I was explaining the subject matter experts at Uberflip, we had this campaign that I ran that was called Uberflip.
Unlock.
And it was like, I recorded like one to two minute videos of subject matter experts.
So it was basically everybody on the marketing team kind of pulling back the curtain and showing how they use our platform to create something.
So it's like, hi, I'm our event marketer.
I'm going to show you how I use Uberflip to create an event follow-up landing page.
Or hi, I'm, you know, our demand gen marketer.
I'm going to show you how I use this to, you know, market an ebook.
So being able to use that, we sort of like built a little community around that.
So it was like subscriber based.
And I was sending out like biweekly emails whenever we released a new video.
But that was kind of a really, you know, a way that resonated with people as we, as we sent that out and build out that program.
But that's also something that I've done, like more recently when I was at Arbinger.
So, you know, using our managing director or some of our speakers, it was a leadership development company.
So leverage.

Leveraging a lot of those like voices and those faces that were out in the public doing talks, getting them to do like webinars or even just like short videos to kind of like resonate with people and capture that audience.
And we're starting to do that at Ask.ai too with like more founder brand kind of stuff.
So leveraging like our CEO and talking about like how he's building the product and how like his, you know, experience doing that to kind of like build that community that way.

So I think it's, it's definitely something that it's looked a little bit different in every role for me, but it's, it's sort of the way that I like to sort of like build like the foundations of a content campaign around that.
Yeah, that makes, that makes sense.
I guess for after the call, if there's anything you've written, like recently you could share, that would be really helpful.
Yeah, but I mean, like we, we have like probably just like much more.
resources behind the creation of content than most companies, I imagine.
Like for one, it's kind of like our calling card to show our customers like, hey, if you really invested, this is, you know, what that could look like.
Like we have the, like a full-time video production guy does our podcast, does our workshops.
I mean, I guess our whole team is essentially like filled with writers, but yeah, this role is meant to be like the, like the main writer almost, you know, because we want to go a lot deeper.
Well, I think, I guess all the questions I had, I mean, I guess like what we've been doing is like, there's a little kind of take home where we just like write, write something.
And then we kind of go from there.
It's a pretty short process.
Is there any other questions I can, I can answer just to help you kind of form an opinion, whether this is interesting or not?
Yeah, I think I, I have some.
More questions.
Kind of around like the way that your team works and like the structure.

And then this particular role would be like the content marketer that's like marketing growthx.
Am I understanding that correctly?
Yeah.
So this one would be like basically fully internal, though, like, I don't know.
I mean, we're kind of like, like I have my time spent on clients.
So if you wanted to spend time on clients, you could, you know, you could totally do that.
Like I will say, yeah, I mean, we have workflows for all this stuff.
I think the content for this would be so kind of specialized.
It might be a little bit tough to like use our workflows fully for.
So I could see a world where like you were interested in getting like much deeper into like how we use workflows to create content.
And.
So.

Got it.

Yeah, that's a good question.
It's, I think we're changing it so often.
wouldn't say it's templatized yet.

I guess I don't know what the UI for relevance looks like, but our platform is a little bit more like, almost like clay or like, I mean, if you've used Aerofs, it's like a little bit, I mean, there's different layers to it.
But like, for example, you know, this is like one of our customers.
Let's do articles we're publishing for them.
And then.
We just have like, you know, you have a topic and then there's like steps that it goes through basically.

So like, oh, it's a keyword or a prompt we want to kind of get cited in.
And then that shapes kind of like what we're even writing and what kind of direction and intent and audience that it's for.
Or, and then, you know, we have different steps of like researching, just take this one, for example.
It's like, a lot of it's not formatted very nicely, but like this is, let's see, this is like some rough instruction, but like the first step is doing research.
So it goes out and it finds like all the relevant kind of sources that kind of can inform an article on, what's this on?
Behavioral AI, so this is for like one of our security clients.
And it does a lot of work writing the article.
Yeah.
It does a lot of work adding the links, checking sources, we have like writing guidelines, so it like checks against our writing guidelines.
And then I guess we have some stuff just on like the SEO parts of it.
But almost all my workflows look something like this.
Yeah.
And then I would say like depending on the type of content, we'll have different workflows.
So like the workflow I use for Webflow to create like, we do all their integration docs.
And to create this, it's like totally different, you know, like there's code in here.
There's like a lot of restrictions for like what you can link to.
Like, you know, they're not going to link to some random blog posts that tells you how to integrate these two tools, for example.
Like they only want to like to.
Yeah.
Kind of their own documentation almost.
Like this.
The would look totally different.
Like it's got a big like FAQ section.
Yeah.
Is that the answer to question?
So like that, like the layer that I showed, guess, is the layer of the writer's kind of pilot.
But then underneath all of that, like for example, how that research step works, that's like basically done by our engineering team.
So it's a lot of stuff in code.
Yeah.
It's like a whole separate platform.
Got it.
No, that makes sense.
And then I guess, sorry, have so many questions.
I think like my other question would be kind of around just like the scope of this role in particular.
So it sounds kind of like building and owning the content strategy.
Is that kind of like a fair like assessment?
So like everything from like blogs and like editorial content, but more of like the lead gen content, the community kind of content.
And am I sort of understanding that scope?
Yeah, I would probably.
I feel like the majority of it would be the community content.
Okay.
But I mean, we have so much content we need to create.
So it really depends on like bandwidth a little bit.

So like the community content would be something like, you know, like right now we're doing like a masterclass where such as just taking everything we do for clients and trying to like break that down to teach people.
So there's like a module on how you even come up with the content strategy.

There's a module on like how you measure and like present information, you know, like we're kind of almost creating a course for our own team and then like releasing that because I think everyone's kind of struggling through the same thing.
So for that, it's like, like, I think the work probably looks a lot more like, you know, can we kind of map the right topics?

And then for a given topic, like, hey, who is the best person on the team, whether that's you or like one of the people that lead delivery to like write the module on like, how do you pick the right product?
To track when you're, you know, when you want to, yeah, measure how well you're doing on, on AEO or GEO.
So that, that would, I mean, that one would probably be quite a lot of work.
And then, I mean, we just have so many different types of content.
It's like the workshops, I mean, the workshops mostly do themselves, but like, you know, if you've watched any of them, there's like a slide deck, there's like a, an agenda.
Can we think about like how to make that better?
Can we plan the roadmap?
Can of, uh, workshop guests that for the next like two quarters?
Um, yeah, I'm just thinking through like other types of content we want to produce.
Like we want to do like teardowns.
It's like, Hey, can we tear down like Zapier's SEO strategy and make like a, you know, almost like a report on that and make that available to our community?
Um, like how would you even start to put that together?
You know, like who do you talk to on the team to interview?
Got it.
Got it.
That's.
you.
That's helpful.
Yeah, I think the community piece is also like really interesting.
We did, when I was at Uberflip, we did something that sounds kind of similar where we had like a public facing like certification that kind of was like a lead gen sort of initiative.

And then we also had like a customer facing version that was like once customers on board, that was sort of how they learned the platform and tapping different subject matter experts internally for that.
So it sounds like a similar approach and that worked really well for us.
Yeah, that was really cool.
I feel like people have really been like leveling up what they've been doing here quite a bit.
Like a lot of our competitors are doing this like kind of almost like cohorted courses, you know, or cohorted like, you know, boot camps, which is like super interesting.
Yeah, I've seen those.
Like Clay's, you know, really great at doing that on this.
Yeah.
So we really want to like push the bar on like, and that was a big reason for like detaching this content community from our brand.

Like this isn't even, like for the most part, I mean, you see the same people in the video, so people know, but like it's a totally different brand, you know, like we barely push like the services that we do at all.
Like it's just meant to be like its own thing.
Yeah, that makes sense.
On the competitors piece, who would you say, who would you consider to be like your, your primary competitors?
I think like, I think in the space what's happening is like everything seems to be converging.
So we probably have competitors on two sides.
There's like the services competitors.
So, you know, the, the agencies that do what we do.
So like animals is a big one.
They're like a really big SEO agency.
There's one called
We actually know them personally.

know the founder and like they just do a really good job just doing this work kind of manually, you know, but they have a great brand and, you know, they have really good thought leadership and thoughts on the topic.
So that's like a big group of competitors.
And those people are starting to like build tool platforms because they realize that there's writing on the wall for like AI replacing large swaths of what they do.

And then on the other side, we have kind of like platform tool companies that are starting to realize that they don't kind of capture the like human labor part of what their product does, that they're also leaving a ton on the table.
And eventually AI is going to kind of wrap what they do and replace them as well.
So like in that space, there's profound, there's air ops, there's like peak and scrunch, like all these tools that kind of started as either like workflow automation or just like, like AI.
Like they're now starting to build in the pieces where like they can actually go to these companies and be like, hey, we'll just kind of take all this, you know, in-house from what you're doing.
So, yeah, I guess those are like probably the two kind of groups and we're all converging to the same point.
like, hey, what do you need to build to like fully own kind of like this function from your customers, you know, where you can deliver an outcome and not just be like a tool that they use.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss the Managing Editor role at GrowthX for their AI Like Growth community and content marketing initiatives.](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=137.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is building an AI platform for marketers, currently operating as a growth agency with 70+ customers and $13M revenue](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=172.0)
  - [The role involves owning content strategy for GrowthX's community, including workshops, courses, and educational content on AI-driven marketing](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1771.0)
  - [GrowthX emphasizes transparency and "building in public", sharing their processes to build trust while maintaining a competitive edge](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=895.0)
  - [The ideal candidate has experience creating engaging, educational content for marketers and facilitating expert-driven content creation](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1121.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Business Model and Services

  - [Operating as a growth agency to prove out AI platform concept](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=199.0)
  - [70+ customers, $13M revenue, focusing on awareness through SEO and AI-driven content creation](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=215.0)
  - [Services include SEO content, developer docs, and integration guides at scale](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=262.0)
  - [Leveraging AI with expert oversight to create efficient content "machines"](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=244.0)

### AI Like Growth Community

  - [Educational platform teaching AI-driven marketing techniques](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=293.0)
  - [Content includes workshops, podcasts, courses, and masterclasses](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=327.0)
  - [Currently has 60-70 paying members at $200/year, with plans to increase pricing](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=392.0)
  - [Separate branding from GrowthX services to focus on pure education](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1983.0)

### Managing Editor Role Responsibilities

  - [Own content strategy for AI Like Growth community](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1771.0)
  - [Facilitate creation of educational content, including writing and coordinating with subject matter experts](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1121.0)
  - [Potential involvement in client work to understand GrowthX workflows](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1538.0)
  - [Focus on creating engaging, in-depth content that goes beyond surface-level SEO articles](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2752.0)

### GrowthX Team Structure

  - [Organized around "offers" rather than traditional marketing functions](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2418.0)
  - [Key areas: Community, Events, Product (Check That Out), and Marketing](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2556.0)
  - [Community team split between content creation and community management](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2582.0)
  - [Collaboration across teams for events, workshops, and marketing initiatives](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2683.0)

### Content Creation Approach

  - [Emphasis on transparency and "building in public"](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=895.0)
  - [Leveraging client work and internal expertise to create valuable, real-world examples](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1121.0)
  - [Balancing oversharing with maintaining competitive advantage](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=895.0)
  - [Focus on creating trust through education, even if some customers attempt to replicate processes](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=895.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Melissa to send writing samples showcasing in-depth, educational content](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2743.0)
  - [GrowthX to provide a writing assignment (600-700 words) on a marketing-related topic](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2710.0)
  - [Schedule a panel interview to discuss the writing assignment](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2710.0)
  - [Melissa to further explore the AI Like Growth community content](https://fathom.video/share/xHjxVAP5kgzyz17PqBK-L6F5YKDt42zJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2829.0)

action_items.description: Email Melissa writing assignment topic + instructions
Review AI Like Growth community; if paywalled, ask Jason for access
